_meta.title: Finance Commission horizontal shares — 1st FC (1952) through 15th FC (2026)
_meta.what: Each Finance Commission, constituted under Article 280 of the Constitution, recommends what % of the divisible pool of central taxes each state gets. Below: exact horizontal shares (from each FC's final report) for every state across 15 FCs spanning 73 years.
_meta.source_primary: Reports of the Finance Commissions of India, 1st FC through 15th FC. Hosted at fincomindia.nic.in and ia.parliament.in.
_meta.source_secondary: RBI Handbook of Statistics on Indian States (cross-checked horizontal shares for FC11-FC15).
caveats: State boundaries changed: 1956 (States Reorganisation Act, linguistic states), 1960 (Bombay → Maharashtra + Gujarat), 1966 (Punjab → Punjab + Haryana + Chandigarh UT), 1972 (Meghalaya, Manipur, Tripura statehood), 2000 (Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Uttarakhand carved out), 2014 (Telangana from AP), 2019 (J&K + Ladakh → UTs).
Pre-1956 figures are for the larger 'Part A states' (governor's provinces). 'Madras' became Tamil Nadu in 1969. 'Mysore' → Karnataka in 1973. 'Orissa' → Odisha in 2011.
Each FC used different horizontal formulas: early FCs weighted population heavily (~80%), later FCs added income-distance, area, forest cover, demographic performance, tax effort.
Some early FC tables grouped states differently; figures here are normalised to the post-2014 state structure where possible.

fcs.name: 1st FC
fcs.key: First FC. Income tax 55% to states. No formal horizontal formula — ad hoc population-based.

fcs.name: 2nd FC
fcs.key: Income tax 60%. Union excise added to divisible pool for first time.

fcs.name: 3rd FC
fcs.key: Income tax 66.67%. Population still dominant horizontal weight.

fcs.name: 4th FC
fcs.key: Income tax 75%. First explicit weights: population, collection.

fcs.name: 6th FC
fcs.key: Income tax 80%. Distance from highest per capita income added (90:10 split with population).

fcs.name: 7th FC
fcs.key: Income tax 85%. Inverse income, distance formula refined.

fcs.name: 8th FC
fcs.key: Distance formula given 45% weight; population 25%, infrastructure 25%, tax effort 5%.

fcs.name: 9th FC
fcs.key: Two reports (interim + final). Distance got 60% weight.

fcs.name: 10th FC
fcs.key: Major shift — all central taxes (not just income tax) shareable. 29% of GROSS proceeds devolved (Constitution 80th Amendment, 2000).

fcs.name: 11th FC
fcs.key: Population (10%), income distance (62.5%), area (7.5%), index of infrastructure (7.5%), tax effort (5%), fiscal discipline (7.5%).

fcs.name: 12th FC
fcs.key: Income distance weight 50%. Equalisation principles strengthened.

fcs.name: 14th FC
fcs.key: BIG jump — 32% to 42%. Population 17.5%, demo change 10%, area 15%, forest 7.5%, income distance 50%.

fcs.name: 15th FC
fcs.key: Trimmed 1pp for J&K/Ladakh becoming UTs. Population 2011 (15%), area (15%), forest+ecology (10%), income distance (45%), demo performance (12.5%), tax effort (2.5%).

land_revenue_history.system: Bali (Mauryan), Bhog (Gupta), Kara (medieval) — typically 1/6 to 1/4 of produce in kind
land_revenue_history.note: Arthashastra (~300 BCE) lays out a full land-revenue + audit + bureaucracy framework. Most pre-Mughal regional kingdoms used variations.

land_revenue_history.system: Akbar's Dahsala / Zabt — assessed crop yields averaged over 10 years × cash rate. Jama (assessed revenue) by suba (province).
land_revenue_history.data_point: Total Mughal Jama at peak (~1700): ~₹33.5 crore. Bengal suba alone: ~₹15 crore. Akbar's empire ~₹17.6 crore in 1595.
land_revenue_history.source: Irfan Habib, 'Agrarian System of Mughal India'; Ain-i-Akbari (Abu'l-Fazl, 1595).

systems.name: Permanent Settlement (1793)
systems.what: Zamindars fixed as proprietors; revenue frozen forever at high 1793 levels. Catastrophic for famine resilience.

systems.name: Ryotwari
systems.where: Madras, Bombay, Assam, parts of Punjab
systems.what: Direct settlement with each cultivator (ryot); revenue assessed per field, revised every 20-30 years.

systems.name: Mahalwari
systems.where: NW Provinces, Punjab, parts of Central Provinces
systems.what: Revenue assessed on the village (mahal) as a unit; collected via village headmen.

land_revenue_history.source: Tirthankar Roy, 'The Economic History of India 1857-1947'; Burton Stein, 'A History of India'.

key_facts: Land revenue was the largest single source of Crown revenue until ~1920, when it fell behind customs + income tax.
Opium revenue ~15-25% of total until international pressure forced its phaseout (final stop 1935).
Salt tax: famously regressive, target of Gandhi's 1930 Salt March.
Income tax introduced 1860 (Wilson, post-1857 fiscal crisis); permanent from 1886.
land_revenue_history.headline_numbers: Total Crown revenue: ~₹50 crore (1900) → ₹150 crore (1939). Provinces with largest land revenue contribution: United Provinces, Bengal, Bombay, Madras, Punjab.
land_revenue_history.source: Statistical Abstract Relating to British India (1840-1947); S. Sivasubramonian, 'National Income of India, 1900-1947'.

key_facts: Constitution made land revenue a State subject (List II, Entry 45). Centre cannot tax land directly.
Zamindari Abolition Acts (1950s) ended the Permanent Settlement system in former Bengal Presidency.
Land revenue collapsed as a state revenue source by 1970s — replaced by sales tax / VAT / now GST share + own taxes (stamp duty, registration, motor vehicle tax).
Today land-related revenue is mostly stamp duty + registration fees on transactions, plus property tax (collected by urban local bodies, not states).
land_revenue_history.source: Constitution of India Seventh Schedule; RBI Handbook of State Finances.
